hypothesis: Assets with positive medium-term trends tend to keep performing, because capital moves slowly, investors underreact, and macro regimes persist.
portfolio_rule: Hold every asset with positive 12-1 momentum, equal-weighted; park the rest in cash.
expected_strength: Avoids prolonged drawdowns by stepping aside

hypothesis: Assets with stronger relative performance tend to keep leading the weaker ones.
portfolio_rule: Select the top 30% of assets by relative momentum, equal-weighted; cash if none qualify.

hypothesis: Assets with extreme short-term losses partially rebound because of overreaction, liquidity pressure, or temporary positioning.
portfolio_rule: Hold the most oversold assets (z <= -1), equal-weighted; cash if none qualify.

hypothesis: Momentum exposure becomes steadier when position size is reduced during high-volatility periods.
formula: scale[t,i] = min(1, target_vol / realized_vol_63[t,i]); weight = momentum_weight * scale
portfolio_rule: Take momentum positions, then scale each by its volatility target; unused weight to cash.

hypothesis: Combining simple signals reduces dependence on any single fragile signal.
formula: ensemble_score[t,i] = mean(standardized scores of the 4 signals)
portfolio_rule: Hold every asset with a positive average score, equal-weighted; cash otherwise.
expected_weakness: Failure modes are harder to read
